comments.text: I think I can use the flow method used in one of the proofs of [1196] to reprove Behrend's bound $\sum_{n \in A; n \leq x} \frac{1}{n} \ll \frac{\log x}{\sqrt{\log\log x}}$ when $A$ consists of natural numbers (incidentally, the constraint $n \in A$ is currently missing in the statement of Behrend's bound). On the squarefree numbers $n \leq x$, create a flow by setting $w(np \mapsto n) = \frac{1}{np} \frac{\omega(n)!}{(\sum_{p \leq x} 1/p)^{\omega(n)+1}}$, where $\omega(n)$ is the number of prime factors of $n$. At any $1 < n \leq x$, the net outflow of $n$ is $\frac{1}{n} \frac{\omega(n)!}{(\sum_{p \leq x} 1/p)^{\omega(n)}}$, which is at least the net inflow; and at $1$ the net inflow is at most $1$. The discrete divergence theorem then gives the inequality $$ \sum_{n \in A, n \leq x, \mathrm{squarefree}} \frac{1}{n} \frac{\omega(n)!}{(\sum_{p \leq x} 1/p)^{\omega(n)}} \leq 1. \tag{1}$$ From Stirling's formula and Mertens' theorem one has $\frac{\omega(n)!}{(\sum_{p \leq x} 1/p)^{\omega(n)}} \gg \frac{\sqrt{\log\log x}}{\log x}$, giving Behrend's inequality for the squarefree $n$. (This gain over Behrend is analogous to the gain of the LYM inequality over Sperner's inequality.) The remaining $n$ can be dealt with by factoring out the maximal square factor $d^2$ and achieving a similar bound (with a gain of $1/d^2$) for each such value of $d$. The Stirling formula also produces a factor of $\sqrt{2\pi}$ that also appears in a refinement of Behrend's bound proven by Erdos, Sarkozy, and Szemeredi. This is probably not a coincidence. I think the same sort of argument works to give the more general estimate of Ahlswede, Khatchatrian, and Sarkozy that $\sum_{n \in A, y \leq n \leq yx} \frac{1}{n} \ll \frac{\log x}{\sqrt{\log\log x}}$ for any $x,y \geq 1$ and primitive set $A$ of integers; one now has to restrict $\omega(n)$ to only count prime factors up to $x$. EDIT: I can remove the squarefree restriction in (1), thus giving the Erdos-Sarkozy-Szemeredi bound $\sum_{n \in A; n \leq x} \frac{1}{n} \leq (\sqrt{2\pi}+o(1)) \frac{\log x}{\sqrt{\log\log x}}$ from Stirling and Mertens. Here it is convenient to go back to the Markov chain approach. Consider a chain that starts at $1$ and jumps from $n$ to $nq$ with probability $\frac{1/q}{\sum_{q' \leq x} 1/q'}$ where $q,q'$ are restricted to be prime powers that are at most $x$. This chain hits $A$ at most once, but each $n$ is hit with probability at least $\frac{1}{n} \frac{\omega(n)!}{(\sum_{q \leq x} 1/q)^{\omega(n)}}$, by considering the $\omega(n)!$ ways to split $n$ as the product of distinct prime powers. The claim follows. UPDATE: Actually it looks like one doesn't quite get the sharp constant $\sqrt{2\pi}$ by this approach.
